content.text: Every HTTP exchange carries two machine-readable labels that communicate intent and outcome: the
(also called the HTTP verb) that declares what operation the client wants to perform,
— a three-digit integer in the response — that reports whether and how the operation succeeded.
In agentic AI systems, every call to an LLM API, every tool invocation over REST,
every webhook dispatch, and every health check travels over HTTP.
Agents that ignore status codes end up retrying operations that will never succeed,
failing to retry operations that should be retried, and duplicating side effects on non-idempotent requests.
HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is the application-layer protocol that underpins most web APIs.
, an empty separator line, and optionally a
headers, and an optional body. The method and status code are the two fields
that agent error-handling logic reads on every call.
There are eight standardized methods. The most important for agent developers are:
(submit data or invoke an action — most LLM completions, tool invocations),
(headers only — check existence without transferring the body), and
(discover supported methods — used in CORS preflight).
Status codes are grouped into five families:
The combination of method and status code creates a compact, protocol-level contract between
client and server that every HTTP library, proxy, CDN, and firewall on the network understands.
This shared vocabulary is what makes it possible to write generic retry, caching, and
error-handling middleware that works across unrelated APIs.
Agent orchestrators are HTTP clients making dozens or hundreds of calls per task.
Unlike a human who reads an error message on screen, an agent loop must classify the
outcome of each HTTP call programmatically and decide whether to retry, abort, fix
authentication, or slow down. Misclassifying a
The method/status vocabulary is the shared contract that makes automatic error-handling tractable.
Method selection also has operational consequences beyond semantics.
for a state-changing operation like sending a message or debiting an account
seems harmless until an intermediate proxy or CDN caches the response and replays it,
or a browser prefetch fires a deletion without user intent.
for a pure read-only operation means sacrificing HTTP-layer
caching, increasing latency for any retrieval pipelines that serve your agent.
Status codes become the branching logic of an autonomous agent.
A production agent needs, at minimum:
to surface authentication issues without retrying;
transient errors and apply exponential backoff;
errors and surface them rather than looping endlessly.
Getting this right prevents cascading failures and wasted LLM tokens.
Using HTTP methods and status codes with their intended semantics provides:
— any HTTP client, proxy, or middleware can interpret a status code and route or cache correctly without application-specific knowledge.
— safe methods (GET, HEAD) may be retried, cached, and prefetched freely; unsafe methods signal possible side effects to all intermediaries.
— idempotent methods (GET, PUT, DELETE) may be retried on network failure without risking duplicate state changes, simplifying agent retry logic.
— GET responses may be cached by HTTP proxies, browser caches, and CDN edges, eliminating redundant calls for repeated reads.
— HEAD requests check resource existence and read headers without transferring the body, saving bandwidth for large resources.
— the same retry, logging, and circuit-breaker middleware works across unrelated APIs because the status vocabulary is universal.
— browser-based agents can query allowed methods on a cross-origin endpoint before committing to a real request.
— many APIs, including most LLM providers, use POST for all requests regardless of safety. This loses REST-level caching and makes idempotency handling the client's responsibility.
— HTTP defines status codes but not error payloads. Each API invents its own JSON error schema, so an agent must parse API-specific error formats in addition to inspecting status codes.
but include an error field in the JSON body, violating HTTP semantics. Agents that only check the status code misclassify these as successes.
from one API means 'malformed JSON'; from another it means 'invalid parameter values'. Retry policy must be calibrated per API, not globally applied.
— older firewalls and proxies block PUT, PATCH, and DELETE, forcing clients to use POST with an
header, which breaks the protocol contract.
header is widely adopted (Stripe, Anthropic, OpenAI) but not part of the HTTP standard; servers that do not implement it cannot guarantee safe POST retries.
An HTTP/1.1 request begins when the client opens a TCP (and, in HTTPS, TLS) connection
to the server, then sends: a request line (METHOD SP Request-URI SP HTTP/1.1 CRLF),
header fields, a blank line, and optionally a body. The server sends back a status line
(HTTP/1.1 SP Status-Code SP Reason CRLF), headers, a blank line, and a body.
In HTTP/2 this is framed differently (binary frames, HPACK header compression, stream multiplexing),
but the semantic model — method + URL in, status code out — is identical.
Each HTTP method has three binary properties.
means the method has no intended side effects: the server state should be the same before and after.
means that applying the method N times produces the same server state as applying it once (the response may differ, but the side effect does not compound).
means HTTP intermediaries may store and reuse the response.
Read tool metadata, health checks, fetch documents
Check resource existence / ETag without body transfer
Replace agent config, update stored resource
Partial update of agent state or session
Cancel async jobs, remove sessions, deregister webhooks
The first digit of a status code encodes the semantic family.
codes are informational; the most useful is
means a new resource was created (often returned with a
signals the request is queued for async processing;
means success with an empty body (delete, webhook acknowledgements).
codes indicate redirection; most HTTP libraries follow them automatically.
codes indicate client-side errors — the request is wrong or the client lacks permission.
codes indicate server-side errors that may be transient.
It sits in the 4xx range, but unlike
header specifies either a delay in seconds or an HTTP date
after which the client may retry. Agents that treat all 4xx codes as fatal will break on any API with rate limits.
Because POST is neither safe nor idempotent, retrying a POST on network failure can trigger duplicate actions:
send the same email twice, charge a card twice, or enqueue a task twice.
request header (originating from Stripe, now widely adopted including by Anthropic and many payment APIs) provides a solution:
the client generates a unique key per logical operation and sends it with the POST.
If the server receives a subsequent request with the same key within a deduplication window,
it returns the cached response instead of processing the request again.
The key should be generated before the first attempt and persisted in the agent's task state
The diagram below shows the complete flow an agent follows when making an HTTP call:
constructing the request with the correct method and headers, receiving a response with
a status code, and branching to one of three outcomes — parse and continue, rate-limit backoff and retry, or abort with a typed exception.

attrs.alt: HTTP Methods and Status Codes — Agent Request / Response Flow

content.text: The three decision paths map directly to agent error policy.
When the status is 2xx the agent parses the response body and continues the task loop.
When the status is 429 the agent reads
waits, and resends the identical request — with the same
When the status is a non-retryable 4xx the agent raises a typed exception with the error details.
When the status is a 5xx the agent backs off and retries up to a configured maximum,
then escalates to a failure exception if exhausted.

HTTP method and status-code handling applies to every external API call an agent makes. The method choice is usually dictated by the API contract, but the following guidance captures the common patterns:
for read-only operations with no side effects: fetching available tool schemas,
reading knowledge base articles, polling a job status endpoint, checking an LLM provider's model list.
GET responses may be cached by the HTTP layer, saving round trips for repeated reads.
for creating resources, submitting data, or invoking LLM inference endpoints.
All major LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Mistral, Cohere) expose their completions and embeddings APIs as POST.
when the POST triggers an irreversible side effect that may be retried.
when updating stored resources and the API supports it.
updates a subset. Both may be retried safely (PUT is idempotent; PATCH may not be).
for canceling async jobs, removing conversation sessions, or deregistering webhooks.
DELETE is idempotent — a second DELETE on a non-existent resource returns 404 but has no additional side effect.
before downloading large resources (models, data files) to check the
When NOT to build a custom status-code handler:
When using an official SDK that already implements retry, rate-limit handling, and error classification — the Python
libraries both do this. Build custom handling only for APIs
that lack a maintained SDK or whose behavior deviates from what the SDK expects.
Implementing correct HTTP handling in an agent involves three layers:
HTTP client configuration (timeouts, connection pooling, HTTP/2),
request construction (correct method, headers, body, idempotency key), and
Choose a robust HTTP client library
that supports connection pooling, explicit timeouts, automatic redirect following, and ideally HTTP/2.
is recommended for modern Python agents.
— a connect timeout (2–5 s) and a read timeout that matches the slowest expected response (e.g. 120 s for long LLM generations). Never leave the timeout at the library default (often infinite).
for any POST that triggers a side effect and may be retried. Use a UUID generated before the first attempt; persist it in task state.
Classify the status code before accessing the body
— 2xx to parse, 204 to skip body parsing, 429 to backoff, 4xx or 5xx to dispatch to typed exceptions.
. Jitter prevents thundering-herd retries when many agents hit the same rate limit simultaneously.
over self-computed backoff when the header is present — it encodes the server's own knowledge of when capacity will be available.
(async) across multiple calls to avoid per-call TCP+TLS handshake overhead.
Example 1 — Calling an LLM completion API (Python)
A minimal but correctly structured Python function that calls the OpenAI chat completions endpoint, handles the most common status codes, and is easy to extend with retry logic:
Example 2 — Idempotent tool invocation with retry (JavaScript)
-based function that sends a POST with an
A production multi-step agent that orchestrates searches, summarizations, and email drafts
will make HTTP calls to at minimum three or four external APIs in a single task:
an LLM API for generation, a search API for retrieval, a calendar API to check availability,
and a CRM API to look up contacts. Each of these APIs has different rate limits,
different status-code behaviors, and different error schemas.
The following shows a production-calibrated client for the Anthropic Messages API
that handles the specific status codes Anthropic returns, including
header (required by Anthropic), the 120 s read timeout
(needed for long generations), and the explicit handling of
(an Anthropic-specific overload code that behaves like
when present and otherwise uses exponential backoff with a cap.
how to surface each failure class to the user.
is a 4xx status code, but unlike
, it is designed to be retried.
Agents that group all 4xx codes as permanent client errors will bail out on every
rate limit, breaking the entire agent run. Similarly,
are retryable 4xx codes that some agents treat as fatal.
The correct rule: classify by specific code, not by family alone.
Retrying POST without an idempotency key
If an agent retries a POST that sends an email, charges a card, or enqueues a task
, it may trigger the action twice.
Without the key, the server has no way to detect the duplicate.
Always generate and persist a stable key per logical operation before the first attempt,
and reuse the same key on every retry of that operation.

# ---- MISTAKE 1: Treating all 4xx as non-retryable ----

# WRONG — 429 is a 4xx but must be retried
if resp.status_code >= 400:
    raise RuntimeError("API error")

# CORRECT — classify before deciding
if resp.status_code == 429:
    time.sleep(float(resp.headers.get("Retry-After", "5")))
    # then retry the same request
elif resp.status_code in (401, 403):
    raise PermissionError("auth failure — not retryable")
elif 400 <= resp.status_code < 500:
    raise ValueError(f"bad request: {resp.text}")  # not retryable


# ---- MISTAKE 2: Calling .json() on a 204 response ----

# WRONG — 204 has an empty body; this raises JSONDecodeError
data = resp.json()

# CORRECT
if resp.status_code == 204:
    return None   # success, no body
return resp.json()


# ---- MISTAKE 3: Sending API key in query parameter ----

# WRONG — logged verbatim by proxies, CDNs, and server access logs
resp = httpx.get(f"https://api.example.com/data?api_key={secret}")

# CORRECT — key travels inside an encrypted header, not the URL
resp = httpx.get(
    "https://api.example.com/data",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {secret}"},
)
it means the server's rate-limit window resets in 60 seconds.
An agent that retries after only 1 second will receive another
header is the server telling the client exactly when to retry; ignoring it
Check both status code and body for errors.
with an error field in the JSON body. Always validate the application-level response as well as the HTTP status.
Map HTTP semantics to exception types:
for other 4xx. This makes the calling code clean and testable.
Set explicit timeouts on every HTTP call.
Without a timeout, a stalled LLM API call blocks the agent indefinitely. A 2–5 s connect timeout and an API-appropriate read timeout (30–120 s) are sane defaults.
Log method, URL path, status code, and elapsed time for every call.
This is the single most valuable debugging aid for agent HTTP failures in production. Avoid logging the full URL if it contains tokens in query parameters.
An agent making many sequential calls should reuse the same TCP connection via
, not open a new connection per call. Connection setup adds 50–300 ms per call over TLS.
Use its value rather than a shorter self-computed delay. Ignoring it can worsen rate limits for all clients sharing the same rate-limit bucket.
Never send API keys in URL query parameters.
Query strings are logged verbatim by proxies, CDNs, load balancers, and server access logs. Always use the
Test with a real API in CI.
Status code handling is easiest to validate against a real or recorded HTTP exchange; mocking at the wrong level misses real-world edge cases such as 204 with a non-empty body or 429 without a
Each new TCP+TLS connection adds 50–300 ms of latency due to the three-way handshake and TLS negotiation.
An agent making 50 HTTP calls per task can reduce total latency by several seconds by reusing
connections via HTTP/1.1 keep-alive or HTTP/2 multiplexing.
across multiple calls; never instantiate a new client per request.
Async HTTP for concurrent tool calls
An agent that calls multiple tool APIs simultaneously — a search API, a database lookup,
and a weather API in parallel — should use async HTTP to avoid serializing the calls.
Sequential calls to three APIs each taking 300 ms costs 900 ms; concurrent calls cost ~300 ms.

import asyncio
import httpx

async def gather_tool_results(calls: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    """Execute multiple tool API calls concurrently and collect results.

    Each call is a dict: {url, body, headers}.
    Handles per-request success/failure without aborting the entire batch.
    """
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client:
        tasks = [
            client.post(call["url"], json=call["body"], headers=call["headers"])
            for call in calls
        ]
        responses = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)

    results = []
    for resp in responses:
        if isinstance(resp, Exception):
            results.append({"error": str(resp), "ok": False})
        elif resp.status_code == 200:
            results.append({**resp.json(), "ok": True})
        elif resp.status_code == 204:
            results.append({"ok": True})
        else:
            results.append({"error": f"HTTP {resp.status_code}", "ok": False})
    return results

# Usage:
# calls = [
#     {"url": "https://search.api/search", "body": {"q": "AI"}, "headers": auth},
#     {"url": "https://weather.api/now",   "body": {"loc": "NYC"}, "headers": auth},
# ]
# results = asyncio.run(gather_tool_results(calls))
HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple requests over a single TCP connection, eliminating head-of-line blocking.
When an agent makes concurrent requests to the same host, HTTP/2 can deliver all of them in parallel
over one connection with lower overhead than opening multiple HTTP/1.1 connections.
Most major LLM APIs (Anthropic, OpenAI, Mistral) support HTTP/2.
library negotiates HTTP/2 automatically via ALPN when the

# pip install "httpx[http2]"
import httpx

# HTTP/2 is negotiated via ALPN; the library upgrades automatically
with httpx.Client(http2=True, timeout=30.0) as client:
    resp = client.get("https://api.anthropic.com/v1/models",
                      headers={"x-api-key": "sk-ant-..."})
    print(resp.http_version)  # "HTTP/2" when supported by the server
    print(resp.json())
Agentic workloads tend to burst — many requests submitted in a short window.
Proactive rate-limit management tracks a sliding window of recent calls and inserts deliberate delays
before the limit is hit, rather than reacting to
This is more efficient because reactive backoff wastes the full exponential delay,
while proactive shaping typically requires only a small, predictable delay per call.
Many agent frameworks implement a token-bucket or sliding-window counter per API endpoint.
— async-first, HTTP/2 support, rich timeout control, type-annotated. The recommended choice for modern Python agents. Supports both
— synchronous only, no HTTP/2 natively, but extremely widely used and well-understood. Pair it with
for declarative retry logic. Suitable for simple scripts and tools.
— pure async HTTP client and server. High-concurrency agents benefit from its mature event-loop integration; slightly more verbose API than httpx.
Python packages both wrap httpx and implement retry, rate-limit handling, and error classification internally. Always prefer them over raw HTTP for their respective APIs.
(native) — built into browsers and Node.js 18+. No built-in retry; wrap it or use a small library.
— popular in Node.js; request/response interceptors make it easy to add global retry and auth logic.
— tiny Fetch-based client with built-in retry, hooks, and full TypeScript types. Excellent for TypeScript-native agent code.
for Node.js provide the same production-grade handling as their Python equivalents.
— command-line HTTP clients for testing endpoints.
command is more readable than curl for JSON APIs:
— intercepts and inspects the HTTP traffic of a running agent process. Use it to observe exact headers, status codes, and bodies when debugging production issues.
— GUI tools for testing HTTP endpoints manually before writing agent code. Useful for exploring an unfamiliar API's status-code behavior.
— full packet capture; use when TLS-decryption is needed or when debugging at the TCP connection level (connection pooling, keep-alive, HTTP/2 frames).
HTTP REST is not the only protocol an agent might use. The comparison below covers the most common alternatives in the agent/MCP ecosystem:
LLM APIs, tool calls, REST resources
POST for all queries and mutations
HTTP 200 always; errors embedded in body
Real-time agent events, live model output
HTTP 200 + error object in JSON body
REST/HTTP is the dominant choice for LLM and tool APIs because it is universally supported,
well-tooled, and understood by every layer of the network stack.
gRPC is used for internal microservices where Protobuf efficiency and bidirectional streaming matter.
JSON-RPC is the wire format underlying the Model Context Protocol (MCP),
so agents integrating MCP servers will encounter it alongside standard REST calls.
Every HTTP call from an agent must use HTTPS (TLS 1.2+).
TLS encrypts the entire HTTP payload including headers, so API keys in the
Never fall back to plain HTTP for API calls, even in development environments —
the habits built in dev tend to leak into production.
to work around certificate errors is a common but serious mistake.
It opens the connection to man-in-the-middle attacks where a proxy can intercept, read,
and modify every request — including API keys, model inputs, and tool outputs.
Fix the underlying certificate issue (install the correct CA bundle, or configure
with the right root certificates) rather than disabling verification.
Keep credentials out of URLs and logs
header is protected by TLS but is sometimes forwarded by HTTP redirects
and may appear in server-side access logs if the logging configuration is not careful.
Do not store API keys in environment variables that are printed to stdout in debug mode.
In production, source secrets from a secret manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GCP Secret Manager)
and log only a prefix of the key for audit purposes, never the full value.
Idempotency keys make state-changing operations resilient to network failures, process restarts,
and deployment rollbacks. Generate a UUID per logical operation before the first HTTP attempt,
persist it in the agent's task state (database or durable queue), and resend it on every retry.
If the server returns a cached result, the agent receives the same outcome without duplicate side effects.
This pattern is especially critical for payment, email-send, and message-enqueue tool calls.
A circuit breaker wraps the HTTP client and monitors recent failure rates.
When failures exceed a threshold (e.g. five consecutive
the breaker opens and rejects calls immediately without hitting the network.
This prevents an overloaded or failing downstream API from consuming all agent resources
and propagating failures through the entire orchestration.
in JavaScript both implement retry and circuit-breaker patterns
that compose cleanly with the HTTP client layer.
Think of the HTTP method as the
: '200 Done', '404 Not found', '429 Slow down', '500 My fault'.
An agent's error-handling layer is simply a structured decision tree over status codes:
success → proceed, rate-limited → wait, transient server error → retry,
client error → fix or abort, auth error → surface to user.
The safety / idempotency distinction maps to a practical retry rule:
safe methods may always be retried
idempotent methods may be retried with the same arguments
non-idempotent POST may only be retried with an Idempotency-Key
If you remember only one thing about HTTP in agents, let it be the 429/5xx retry rule:
these are the two families of status codes that indicate 'try again later',
and handling them correctly is what separates a fragile agent from a production-ready one.

GET reads (safe + cacheable), POST creates or acts (not idempotent), PUT replaces (idempotent), PATCH updates partially, DELETE removes (idempotent). Choose the method that matches the operation's semantics.
Status code families are the agent's error classification API:
2xx success, 3xx redirect (usually auto-followed), 4xx client error, 5xx server error. Always classify the specific code, not just the family.
429 Too Many Requests is always retryable:
it sits in the 4xx range but must be retried. Read the Retry-After header and honor it rather than using a shorter computed delay.
Non-idempotent POST requests need an Idempotency-Key:
generate a UUID per logical operation before the first attempt, persist it, and resend on retry. Without it, retrying can trigger duplicate side effects.
Never call .json() on a 204 response and never put API keys in URLs:
204 has an empty body (guard before parsing); query strings are logged by every network intermediary (use the Authorization header).
a missing timeout lets a stalled upstream block the agent indefinitely. Use a short connect timeout (2–5 s) and a read timeout matched to the slowest expected response.
Use connection pooling for multi-call tasks:
instantiate one httpx.Client (or AsyncClient) per agent run and reuse it across all calls. Per-call client instantiation wastes 50–300 ms on TCP+TLS setup for each request.
Official provider SDKs already implement this:

the openai and anthropic Python and JavaScript packages handle retry, rate limiting, and error classification. Prefer them over raw HTTP for their respective APIs; build custom handling only for unsupported APIs.
